coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of amoxicillin in aqueous solution with novel fluorescent probes containing boron-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xin
Ren, Yingkun
Ji, Zhen
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: The antibiotic amoxicillin (AMX) was detected sensitively by a novel fluorescence probes composed with boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs). B-CQDs with a 5:1 molar ratio of C atom versus B atom can be synthesized first by mixing citric acid with boric acid as a B source on suitable hydropyrolysis condition. The AMX in an aqueous solution was detected via fluorometry. Results showed that the as-prepared B-CQDs had nattier blue fluorescence, a narrow diameter range, a mean particle size of 2.3 nm, a multigraphite phase structure, and a quantum yield of 30.85%. The B-CQDs as probes can sensitively detect AMX in aqueous solution, and the fluorescence signal of the B-CQDs-AMX system was strengthened linearly with the increase in AMX concentration when in the antibiotic concentration range of 1.43–429.12 μmol·L−1 with limit of detection of 0.825 μmol·L−1. B-CQDs detected AMX mechanism as follows: the probes can be combined with antibiotic via primary hydrogen bond between them, and their combining abilities were enhanced by increasing the temperature of this sensing system in the range of 288–313 K.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence probe
Amoxicillin
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of boron doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs)
The experiment of B-CQDs fluorescent probe sensing AMX in aqueous solution
B-CQDs as fluorescence probes sensing AMX in aqueous solution
The effect of concomitant substances on the fluorescence property of B-CQDs
The mechanism of B-CQDs probe sensing AMX

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11974276 and 21476183), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2014JM2-2012), the Special Funds for Scientific Research of High-level Talents of Ankang University (2015AYQDZR02), and the Scientific Research Plan Projects of Shaanxi Education Department (Nos. 18JK0014 and 19JS003).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11974276 and 21476183 ), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China ( 2014JM2-2012 ), the Special Funds for Scientific Research of High-level Talents of Ankang University ( 2015AYQDZR02 ), and the Scientific Research Plan Projects of Shaanxi Education Department (Nos. 18JK0014 and 19JS003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FL intensity of B-CQDs changed with different factors (a, time; temperature; c, n C: n B; d, pH).
The FL and UV spectra of B-CQDs (a was the FL consecutive excitation spectra of B-CQDs, and b was the EX, EM and UV spectra of B-CQDs).
The SEM image, EDS spectrum (a) and Elemental mapping (b) of B-CQDs.
The particle size distribution (a), HR-TEM images (b), FTIR (c) and XRD spectra of samples.
The full XPS spectrum of B-CQDs (a), O 1s (b), C 1s (c), B 1s (d).
The relationship between the PL spectra of B-CQDs-AMX system and the concentration of AMX (a, the PL spectra of B-CQDs-AMX detection system; b, the linear fitting of (F-F 0) and C AMX).
The effect of co-matters on the fluorescent intensity of B-CQDs (a, metal ions; b, several saccharides and amino acids).
The fitting linears of AMX enhancing the fluorescent intensity of B-CQDs on different temperature condition.
The preparation schematic of the two quantum dots.
The mechanism schematic of AMX sensed by B-CQDs.
The binding constants and thermodynamic parameters of B-CQDs and AMX.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of amoxicillin in aqueous solution with novel fluorescent probes containing boron-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, No. 229 Taibai North Road, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, No. 229 Taibai North Road, Xi'’an, Shaanxi 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xianyang, Shaanxi 712046, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Northwest University, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'’an, Shaanxi 710069, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The antibiotic amoxicillin (AMX) was detected sensitively by a novel fluorescence probes composed with boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs). B-CQDs with a 5:1 molar ratio of C atom versus B atom can be synthesized first by mixing citric acid with boric acid as a B source on suitable hydropyrolysis condition. The AMX in an aqueous solution was detected via fluorometry. Results showed that the as-prepared B-CQDs had nattier blue fluorescence, a narrow diameter range, a mean particle size of 2.3 nm, a multigraphite phase structure, and a quantum yield of 30.85%. The B-CQDs as probes can sensitively detect AMX in aqueous solution, and the fluorescence signal of the B-CQDs-AMX system was strengthened linearly with the increase in AMX concentration when in the antibiotic concentration range of 1.43–429.12 μmol·L−1 with limit of detection of 0.825 μmol·L−1. B-CQDs detected AMX mechanism as follows: the probes can be combined with antibiotic via primary hydrogen bond between them, and their combining abilities were enhanced by increasing the temperature of this sensing system in the range of 288–313 K.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are new-emerging zero-dimension materials [1]. Considering their excellent photoluminescence performance, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility, these materials have drawn considerable attentions and been applied extensively [2,3]. Many inorganic carbon materials and various organic matters can act as precursors to conveniently synthesize CQDs in many ways [4]. Further doping CQDs with other elements have been achieved in the latest research progress. In addition, the optical property, biocompatibility, and quantum yield of doped CQDs can be improved significantly [5,6]. Therefore, CQDs exhibit multifunctionality and thus can be used to image in vitro and in vivo [7], as carrier vehicles to deliver drug and gene [8,9], to probe and detect contaminants in water environment [10], and to degrade harmful and toxic chemicals in photocatalysis [11]. In terms of their predominant photoluminescence property, luminous stability, and photobleaching resistance, CQDs are used widely as biosensors. Topics including several bioactive matters, such as dopamine, H2S, and amino acids [12–14], varieties of metal ions in relation to Hg(II), Cr(XI), Cu(II), and Fe(III) [4,14–17], several hazardous anions involving F−, CN−, S2−, and PO4 3−, some bacterials including Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus [10,18–20], have been reported. Several studies on CQDs have resulted in its remarkable application. However, sensing the fluorescence of antibiotic drugs in water by using CQDs has been rarely reported [21], and especially amoxicillin was detected by CQDs.

As a common antibiotic, amoxicillin (AMX) is used to treat bacterial infections due to their strong bactericidal capabilities [22]. The excessive use of this drug, its abuse and improper application, especially overcommitted in domestic animals and marine lives [23], have led to antibiotic accumulation in water environment and resulted in cross-drug resistance, flora inhibition in waste water, and considerable potential pollution threat [24–26]. Therefore, effectively removing antibiotics in water have become an urgent problem, and detecting AMX in water is a crucial step in AMX removal.

Boron (B) is next to carbon (C) in the periodic table. Thus, the two elements have similarities in structure and physicochemical properties. B-doped CQDs (B-CQDs) may improve their defects and upgrade their photoluminescence properties. Herein, B-CQDs were first prepared with boric acid as a modifier and citric acid as a carbon source through hydropyrolysis. Second, AMX in aqueous solution was sensed by these as-prepared B-CQDs as fluorescent probe, and the selectivity and sensitivity of the B-CQD probes were investigated. The sensing mechanism was also discussed.

All chemicals used in this paper were analytical grade reagents, including citric acid monohydrate, NaOH (Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China), HCl (Xi'an Sanpu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China), Rhodamine B (Beijing Chemical Works, China), Amoxicillin (Zhengzhou Paini Technology, China). Salts such as KNO3, Pb(Ac)2·3H2O, CaCl2, et al., were all >99.99% of purity. Saccharides like glucose, sucrose and fructose were all analytical grade and had >99.99% of purity. Amino acids included L-cysteine, lysine, phenylalanine, serine and alanine were biological grade and their purity were all >98%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of boron doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid monohydrate as C precursor and boric acid as B source were applied to prepare B-CQDs through one-step hydropyrolysis. The detailed preparation process is as follows. A specific amount of citric acid monohydrate and boric acid was mixed with 30 mL of ultrapure water and then transferred into vessel lined with Teflon and kept for hydrothermal reaction in program blast dryer on certain hydrothermal conditions. The products after hydropyrolysis were filtrated with 0.22 μm micropore filter membrane. A faint yellow B-CQD solution with fine hydrosolubility was obtained. Here, the preparation conditions of B-CQDs were optimized, and CQDs with citric acid monohydrate as C source without B were also prepared under the same conditions.

The fluorescence quantum yields of the two QDs were determined with rhodamine B as the reference compound (Q
r = 0.95) [27]. A certain B-CQD solution concentration and 0.01 mol·L−1 rhodamine B was diluted to several concentrations with ultrapure water. The UV absorbance of these B-CQDs was measured at the maximal excitation wavelength of 315 nm for B-CQDs to ensure that the values were ≤ 0.2 (A ≤ 0.2). The fluorescence integral intensities of B-CQDs were then analyzed with a fluorescence spectrometer at the same wavelength. B-CQDs and rhodamine B were diluted to other concentrations, and the steps above were repeated several times. The determination process above can also be used for CQDs. Fluorescence quantum yield was calculated using Eq. (1) as follows [28]:
(1)
Q x
=
n x 2
n r 2
A r
F r
F x
A x
Q r
where Q
x , Q
r , F
x , F
r , A
x , and A
r are the fluorescence quantum yield, fluorescence integral intensity, UV absorbance of determinand and reference compounds; and n is the refractive index of solvent; here, n
x and n
r are same, and their value is 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The experiment of B-CQDs fluorescent probe sensing AMX in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The B-CQD–AMX sensing system was developed with the following steps. First, 1 mL of B-CQD solution was separated as fluorescent probes and added to 10 mL colorimeter tubes. Second, different AMX volumes with 0.238 mmol·L−1 were mixed with these probe solutions. The mixture was then diluted to 10 mL with ultrapure water at ambient temperature, which was designated the B-CQD–AMX sensing system.

The fluorescence property of the sensing system was analyzed by taking these B-CQD–AMX mixtures and measuring their fluorescence intensity with changing AMX concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) of AMX in the sensing system was calculated using Eq. (2) as follows [13]:
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
k
where σ is the standard deviation of blank sample and that of the fluorescence emission intensity of B-CQDs in this paper, and k is the slope of the fitting linears.

The fluorescent intensities of the B-CQD probes that were affected by concomitant substances were analyzed with the following process. In brief, 1 mL of B-CQDs solution was added to 10 mL colorimeter tubes, and the varieties of 25 μL of metal ions (Na+, K+, Ag+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Al3+, and Cr3+) with 0.02 mol·L−1, amino acids (L-cysteine, lysine, phenylalanine, serine, and alanine), and saccharides (glucose, fructose, and sucrose) were then introduced. Every colorimeter tube contained one coexistence substance and 1 mL of B-CQD probe solutions. The mixture was then diluted to 10 mL with ultrapure water to measure their fluorescent intensities.

Detecting AMX mechanism was commenced by B-CQD fluorescent probes as follows: different volumes of AMX solutions with 0.476 mmol·L−1 were added into 10 mL batch colorimeter tubes with 1 mL of B-CQDs. The solution was diluted to 10 mL with ultrapure water. These B-CQD–AMX mixtures were kept in 288, 298, 313, and 323 K water bath for 30 min, and their fluorescent intensity was analyzed at different temperatures to investigate the potential sense mechanism. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed using a Tecnai G’F20 S-TWIN (PerkineElmer Co., USA), and images of B-CQDs were obtained at an operating voltage of 200 kV with a CCD camera. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6390A) with Elemental mapping and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was applied to obtain the SEM images of the samples. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples was carried out by using a Rigaku D/MAX 3 C powder diffractometer (Cu K as radiation source, λ = 1.5406 Å). An X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was used to measure the surface composition of B-CQDs within a depth of <10 nm required a Physical Electronics Quantum (AXIS NOVA, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, UK). The spectroscopy analysis of the samples was accomplished with F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600, Japan) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, PerkineElmer-650, USA).

Different effects on the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs, including hydropyrolysis temperature, time, the mole ratio of the two precursors, and the pH value of the products, are shown in Fig. 1 . This result indicated that increasing the hydropyrolysis time and temperature strengthened the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs as shown in Fig. 1a and b. This result may be because the increased reaction time and high temperature allow the precursors to react fully and increased B-CQDs. Fig. 1c shows that the presence of the most preferable fluorescence intensity when the mole ratio of C and B atoms was 5:1. The formula weight of –B(OH)2 group had the same value with that of –COOH group. B and C atoms in the two groups had similar hybrid orbital, that is, sp 2 structure. Thus, when –B(OH)2 was substituted for –COOH, which resulted in minimal influence on the structure of B-CQDs, and the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength was barely changed with different n C:n B ratios. The hydrophily of boric acid was weaker than that of –COOH, and the water solubility of B-CQDs decreased when additional –COOH groups were replaced by –B(OH)2. Thus, the fluorescence property for B-CQDs was improved when the atom n C:n B ratio was 5:1. As shown in Fig. 1d, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs was high when their pH was adjusted to either strong acid or base because same homogenous charges in solutions can separate B-CQDs effectively and promote their emitting fluorescence.

Therefore, the preparation of B-CQDs was designed ultimately wherein citric acid and boric acid were mixed as the mole ratio of C versus B atom of 5:1 and reacted on the condition of the hydrothermal temperature with 210 °C and kept for 24 h. CQDs that were not doped with B as the comparison products were prepared on the same condition. The extrinsic feature and preparation simulations of two QDs are exhibited in Scheme 1 , where B-CQD solutions appeared to be brown yellow and can emit bright blue light after irradiating with 365 nm ultraviolet lamps. However, CQDs were pale yellow and can emit a calamine blue visible light (vis) on the same irradiation condition. This result showed that B-CQDs can emit a strong blue light.

The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of B-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . When B-CQDs were excited every 10 nm from 275 nm to 355 nm, the corresponding emission spectra are shown in Fig. 2a, where the emission wavelength of B-CQDs showed red shift and also had the dependence on the maximum excitation wavelength, which was similar to N-doped GQDs [29], with 405 nm maximum emission wavelength when excited at 315 nm (Fig. 2b). A clear ultraviolet absorption was also observed in the range of 200–275 nm for B-CQDs, with the maximum absorption value of 205 nm, which may be the n–σ⁎, n–π⁎, and π–π⁎ electronic transitions of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups [30]. Thus, –OH and –COOH exist in the B-CQD structure.

The as-prepared B-CQDs were scanned by SEM, and the acquired image is shown in Fig. 3a, where the insert displayed a rough disperse state of these B-CQDs. The detailed pattern of these B-CQDs is described in Fig. 4 . The EDS spectra of B-CQDs indicated they were composed of C, O, and B, and their elemental mapping also indicated these element distributions in the structure of B-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 3b.

B-CQDs had good dispersity without any agglomeration, as shown in Fig. 4a, and they were quasi-spherical, with <5 nm of dimension and a mean particle size with 2.3 nm. Fig. 4b shows the detailed morphology feature, in which the lattice fringe of B-CQDs had a statistical spacing of 0.25 nm, according to graphite carbon (100) [31]. In the FTIR spectra of the two samples shown in Fig. 4c, a broad strong absorption peak that was located at 3500–3000 cm−1 represented –OH stretching vibration. For B-CQDs, the peaks at 1712 and 1400 cm−1 were attributed to CO stretching vibration and OH in-plane deforming vibration, respectively [32]; particularly, the peaks at 1189 and 1089 cm−1 corresponded to B–OH bending vibration and CB stretching vibration, respectively [33]. For the FTIR spectra of CQDs, the peak at 1681 cm−1 was caused by CO stretching vibration, reduced by 31 cm−1 compared to that of B-CQDs at 1712 cm−1, which might result from the carbonyl groups on the surface of CQDs were hydrogen bonded with the hydroxyl groups distributed next to them, therefore leading to the absorption red shift, whereas in B-CQDs, such kind of association were blocked by the existence of B–OH groups on their surface; but other peaks at 1405, 1214, 1165, and 902 cm−1 were attributed to OH in-plane deforming vibration, CO stretching vibration, CC stretching vibration, and OH out-of-plane deforming vibration [34]. Thus, B atoms have been linked to the quantum dots structure to form CB and B–O–H bonds, and the locations of the FTIR characterization peaks of B-CQDs were also close to those of CQDs. Fig. 4d shows the XRD spectra of B-CQDs and CQDs, in which the characteristic diffraction peak of the former was 2θ at 28.04°, which was close to graphite phase (002) plane, with 27° of the 2θ diffraction angle. The interplanar distance was 0.318 nm, which was calculated with Bragg's Law (2dsinθ = nλ) [35], the value of which was larger slightly than of graphite (020) plane, with the interplanar spacing of 0.273 nm. This result may be due to the additional H atoms existing in B-CQDs. Meanwhile, 2θ at ~20° was the characteristic diffraction peak of CQDs, which was similar to that of N-doped CQDs prepared by Tian [36], and also illustrated that they were consisted of amorphous carbon. Other weak diffraction peaks at the 2θ values of 14.60°, 40.28°, and 57.94° in the XRD spectra of B-CQDs and CQDs, and they might be the derivatives generated from the hydropyrolysis procedure of citric acid.

The full XPS of B-CQDs showed the characteristic energy peaks of O, C, and B in Fig. 5a, and their high-resolution survey scan spectra are displayed in Fig. 5b–d. The characteristic peaks of O 1s at 531.5 and 532.3 eV represented CO and CO, as shown in Fig. 5b [37]. For C 1s, the energy peaks at 283.9, 285.4, 285.2, 287.1, and 288.8 eV were ascribed to CB, C–C/C=C, CO, CO, and –COOH, respectively [36]. The characteristic peaks at 191.8 and 192.7 eV corresponded to BC and BO, as shown in Fig. 5d, that is, approximately to 191.5 and 192.5 eV of B 1s, as reported by Sheng [38]. The XPS analysis also showed that B atoms were implanted into CQDs successfully.

The quantum yields of B-CQDs and CQDs were determined with rhodamine B as the references and calculated using Eq. (1), and their specific values (Q x) were 30.85% and 17.92%, respectively. Hence, the quantum yield is enhanced prominently after CQDs was doped with boron.

ce:section.ce:section-title: B-CQDs as fluorescence probes sensing AMX in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The section above summarized that the as-prepared B-CQDs had excellent photoluminescence performance, whereas AMX aqueous solution had hardly any excitation center of 3D fluorescence in the range of 200–500 nm. Thus, the fluorescence signal of the sensing system composed of B-CQDs and AMX would vary with the introduction of AMX. Fig. 6a shows that the fluorescence emission spectra of B-CQD–AMX detection system changed with different AMX concentrations. Here, the AMX concentration in 10 mL detection system from “a” to “m” was 0, 1.43, 4.77, 23.84, 47.68, 95.36, 143.04, 190.72, 238.40, 86.08, 357.60, 381.44, and 429.12 μmol·L−1. This result showed that the emission spectra of the sensing system were strengthened gradually with AMX concentration increased on the same analytical condition, and their maximum emission wavelength exhibited red shift because increasing AMX concentration may increase the polarity of the sensing system and lead to this change [39]. This phenomenon showed a relationship between the AMX concentration (C AMX) and △F (△F = F − F 0), as shown in Fig. 6b, and a linear equation (△F = 10.868 C AMX + 231.81) can be obtained, including a good R 2 value of 0.9934. Meanwhile, when the AMX concentration was in 1.43–429.12 μmol·L−1, a favorable linear relationship existed between △F and C AMX in the sensing system.

The stability and sensibility of B-CQDs as a fluorescence probe were characterized with the LOD that was calculated using Eq. (1), as shown in the fitted line in Fig. 6b, and the obtained value of LOD was 0.825 μmol·L−1, which was less than the minimum detection value of AMX of 1.43 μmol·L−1, as shown in Fig. 6b. Thus, a low LOD and a wide detection range of AMX were obtained using B-CQDs as the fluorescence probe, which can be used to sense AMX in aqueous solution.

Fig. 6 also indicates that the F/F 0 value of the sensing system was enhanced by increasing the AMX concentration because either AMX can separate B-CQDs effectively and decrease their nonradioactive transition or they as ligands in the B-CQD–AMX system can reduce the defects of B-CQDs and increase their fluorescence property [40]. Increasing the AMX concentration in the detection system had an improved effect on separating B-CQDs and was in favor of additional B-CQDs with excited state going back to ground state under low resistance with strong fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of concomitant substances on the fluorescence property of B-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effect of concomitant substances on the fluorescence property of B-CQDs, we changed the relative F/F 0 of B-CQDs with every coexistence matter, as shown in Fig. 7 . The concentration of every concomitant substance was 10-fold higher than that of B-CQDs. Fig. 7a shows the majority metal ions had minimal effect on the fluorescence property of B-CQDs, but Fe3+ ions depressed the F/F 0 of this probe; when AMX was sensed by B-CQDs, this detection system can be adjusted to alkaline (pH of 9–11), which did not influence the fluorescence property of the probes, as shown in Fig. 1d. Then, Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution can form Fe(OH)3 precipitant that should be removed. Saccharides and amino acids had weak effect on the fluorescence emission of the B-CQD probe, which can be disturbed minimally to sense AMX, as shown in Fig. 7b. Thus, AMX in aqueous solution can be sensed by the B-CQD probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of B-CQDs probe sensing AMX
ce:section.ce:para:
For the sensing system with fluorescence enhancement, the combination of a determinand and a probe molecule can be discussed with Eq. (3), as follows [41]:
(3)
F ∞
−
F 0
F −
F 0
= 1 +
1
K a
·
1
C Q
where F ∞ is the maximum fluorescence intensity of the sensing system after the determinand was added; F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of the blank probe; K a is the combination constant of the determinand and the probe; and C
Q is the concentration of the determinand. In this equation, a straight line can be obtained by C
Q
−1 as x-axis and (F-F 0)−1 as y-axis, and the K
a value was obtained with the slope of the fitted line.

In the mechanism study of AMX that was sensed by B-CQD probe, AMX with 0, 23.84, 47.68, 95.36, 190.72, 238.40, 286.08, 357.60, and 429.12 μmol·L−1 was mixed with the same amount of B-CQDs under different water baths at 288, 298, 313, and 323 K for 30 min. The fluorescence intensity of this sensing system at 323 K was weaker than that at 313 K, which may be because the fluorescence matters were decomposed at high temperature, and the intramolecular energy transforming and the deactivation probability of external conversion increased by the increment in the collision frequency [40]. The fitting results at 288, 298, and 313 K following Eq. (3) are shown in Fig. 8 , and the K a of B-CQDs and AMX at different temperatures were calculated with the slopes of the three corresponding lines, and their R 2 values are listed in Table 1 . The results suggested that B-CQDs can combine with AMX, and the K
a value of <30 increased with increase in temperature from 288 K to 313 K. Hence, further increasing the temperature is in favor of B-CQDs combining with AMX.

The interaction force of a fluorescence probe and a determinand can be illustrated with the enthalpy (△H), entropy (△S), and free energy (△G) of their combination. The change in enthalpy in a reaction is regarded as a constant when the temperature varied slightly. The △H and △S are calculated with Van't Hoff equation expressed in Eq. (4) [33], where a straight line can be fitted roughly by T −1 as x-axis and LnK a as y-axis, in which the slope and the intercept are used to obtain the values of △H and △S. The experimental values of △G can be calculated by substituting △H and △S in Eq. (5) [33].
(4)
Ln
K a
= −
Δ H
RT
+
Δ S
R
(5)
Δ G = Δ H − T Δ S
where K
a is the combination constant and can be obtained using Eq. (3); T is the reaction temperature (K), which were 288, 298, and 313 K in this paper; R is a constant, with the value of 8.314 J·mol−1·K−1. The experimental values of △H, △S, and △G of the B-CQDs probe combining AMX at 288 K, 298 K, and 313 K, respectively, were also listed in Table 1.

The △H and △S values of the B-CQDs combining AMX were 45.536 KJ·mol−1 and 197.89 J·K−1·mol−1, calculated with Eq. (4). The △G were −11.456, −13.435, −15.403 KJ·mol−1 at 288, 298, and 313 K, respectively. The △H was >0, indicating that the course of the B-CQDs probes combining AMX molecules were endothermic, and increasing temperature would be beneficial to AMX sensing. The △G with values of <0 demonstrated that the combining processes were spontaneous. This detection was easier to be achieved by increasing temperature in a known range, which also was in accordance with the change of K
a .

The law of fluorescence probe combining determinand summarized by Jaya [41] is as follows: when △H is <0 and △S is >0, they interact with electrostatic force; when either △H or △S is <0, their interaction is Van der Waals forces; hydrophobic effect with △S < 0 and the intermolecular hydrogen bond with H > 0. Since the △H of the detection process in our research was 45.536 KJ·mol−1, it showed that the B-CQDs probes combining AMX in aqueous solution relied on the intermolecular hydrogen bond. In addition, AMX molecule contains amine, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups, all which can form intermolecular hydrogen bonds with B-CQDs. The sense mechanism was simulated, as depicted in Scheme 2 .

We prepared B-doped CQDs by mixing citric acid and boric acid on the appropriate hydropyrolysis condition. The as-prepared B-CQDs with <5 nm particle size had a mean diameter of 2.3 nm composed of multilattice planes. Their functional groups included not only –COOH, –OH, and CO but also CB bond; the quantum yields of B-CQDs and CQDs were 30.85% and 17.92%, respectively. B-CQDs as a fluorescence probe can sense AMX in aqueous solution sensitively. When AMX was in the range of 1.43–429.12 μmol·L−1, the relationship between the increase in fluorescence intensity of the sensing system and AMX concentration was linear. The B-CQDs probe had a relative selectivity, and their fluorescence property was affected hardly by common metal ions, amino acids, and saccharides. The mechanism of AMX sensed by B-CQD probe also indicated that their combination was spontaneous, and they interacted mainly as intermolecular hydrogen bond. Therefore, B-CQDs are a promising fluorescence probe that can be applied to sense antibiotics in aqueous solution in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part. All the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed. No conflict of interest exists in the submission of this manuscript, and the manuscript is approved by all authors for publication.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11974276 and 21476183), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2014JM2-2012), the Special Funds for Scientific Research of High-level Talents of Ankang University (2015AYQDZR02), and the Scientific Research Plan Projects of Shaanxi Education Department (Nos. 18JK0014 and 19JS003).
